q: Particle physics mainly deals with the study of:
exp: Particle physics studies elementary particles and fundamental forces.

q: Which of the following is a lepton?
exp: Electron belongs to lepton family.

q: Proton is made up of:
exp: Proton composition is uud.

q: Neutron consists of:
exp: Neutron is made of one up and two down quarks.

q: Which particle has zero charge?
exp: Neutron is electrically neutral.

q: Antiparticle of electron is:
exp: Positron has same mass but opposite charge to electron.

q: Which force is responsible for beta decay?
exp: Weak interaction governs beta decay.

q: Photon is the carrier particle of:
exp: Electromagnetic interaction is mediated by photons.

q: Which particle mediates strong nuclear force?
exp: Gluons bind quarks together.

q: A positron has:
exp: Positron is antimatter counterpart of electron.

q: Which of the following is NOT a fundamental particle?
exp: Proton is composite particle made of quarks.

q: Hadrons are particles made of:
exp: Hadrons are composite particles made from quarks.

q: Which hadron belongs to baryon family?
exp: Baryons contain three quarks.

q: Mesons are made up of:
exp: Mesons consist of quark and antiquark.

q: Muon is classified as:
exp: Muon belongs to lepton family like electron.

q: Which interaction has the shortest range?
exp: Weak interaction acts over extremely short distances.

q: Which force binds protons and neutrons inside nucleus?
exp: Strong nuclear force overcomes proton repulsion.

q: Neutrinos are characterized by:
exp: Neutrinos are neutral particles with very small mass.

q: Which particle is exchange particle for weak force?
exp: Weak interactions are mediated by W± and Z bosons.

q: Annihilation occurs when:
exp: Matter-antimatter annihilation converts mass into energy.

q: In electron-positron annihilation, energy is usually released as:
exp: Mass converts into gamma photons.

q: Which of the following is a boson?
exp: Photon is force carrier boson.

q: Quarks possess a property called:
exp: Quarks have flavor and color quantum numbers.

q: Which quark has charge +2/3e?
exp: Up-type quarks carry +2/3 elementary charge.

q: Down quark has charge:
exp: Down-type quarks have charge -1/3e.

q: Which conservation law is obeyed in all particle interactions?
exp: Electric charge is always conserved.

q: The antiparticle of proton is:
exp: Antiproton has same mass but opposite charge.

q: Which particle experiences all four fundamental forces?
exp: Proton participates in gravitational, electromagnetic, weak and strong interactions.

q: The Standard Model explains:
exp: Standard Model describes elementary particles and interactions.

q: Which particle is electrically neutral and belongs to lepton family?
exp: Neutrinos are neutral leptons.

q: Baryons are composed of:
exp: Baryons are three-quark particles.

q: Which of the following is NOT a lepton?
exp: Proton is a baryon, not a lepton.

q: The strong force is strongest at:
exp: Strong force acts within nucleus.

q: Which particle has the smallest mass?
exp: Electron mass is much smaller than nucleons.

q: A pion is an example of:
exp: Pions are mesons made of quark-antiquark pairs.

q: Which interaction is responsible for holding quarks together?
exp: Strong force mediated by gluons binds quarks.

q: Which particle has no rest mass?
exp: Photon is massless and travels at speed of light.

q: The quark model was proposed to explain:
exp: Quark model explains composition of hadrons.

q: Which particle mediates electromagnetic interaction?
exp: Photon is exchange particle for EM force.

q: Assertion (A): Neutrinos rarely interact with matter. Reason (R): Neutrinos are electrically neutral and interact mainly through weak force.
opts: Both A and R are true and R is correct explanation
Both A and R are true but R is not correct explanation
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
exp: Neutrinos interact weakly and have no charge, so interaction probability is very low.

q: Which conservation law explains why charge before and after reaction remains same?
exp: Total electric charge remains constant in all interactions.
